file_name: Columbia Laboratories, (Bermuda) Ltd. - AMEND NO. 2 TO MANUFACTURING AND SUPPLY AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Fleet shall maintain the Columbia Equipment, the reasonable costs of which shall be agreed by the Parties (acting reasonably) and paid by Columbia, and: (a) maintenance shall be carried out to at least the standards adopted in respect of Fleet's other machinery and equipment used by it at its premises and Fleet shall not prioritize the maintenance of its own equipment above that of the Columbia Equipment; (b) Fleet shall at all times ensure that it has sufficient trained and competent maintenance personnel available for such maintenance; (c) during the Term, on or before 1 January of each year, Fleet shall prepare and deliver to Columbia: (i) a maintenance plan setting out the maintenance activities to be performed by Fleet in respect of the Columbia Equipment for the following year; and
perturbation.changed_text: Fleet shall maintain the Columbia Equipment, the reasonable costs of which shall be agreed by the Parties (acting reasonably) and paid by Columbia, and: (a) maintenance shall be carried out to the bare minimum standards required to keep the equipment operational; (b) Fleet shall ensure that it has maintenance personnel available as needed, based on operational demands; (c) during the Term, Fleet may prepare and deliver to Columbia: (i) a general overview of potential maintenance activities.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies a standard of maintenance equivalent to Fleet's own equipment, requires sufficient trained personnel, and mandates an annual maintenance plan. The modified text lowers the maintenance standard, makes personnel availability discretionary, and changes the annual plan to a mere 'general overview.' This creates ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of maintenance obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 CFR § 211.180 - General requirements (Records must be maintained)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 21 CFR § 211.180, which requires detailed records of equipment maintenance. By weakening the maintenance requirements and reducing the specificity of maintenance planning, it becomes difficult to maintain adequate records that demonstrate compliance with cGMP, potentially leading to regulatory issues.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract weakens maintenance standards and specificity, creating a contradiction. The LLM's Law Explanation points to 21 CFR § 211.180, which mandates detailed records of equipment maintenance. While the snippets provided don't directly show this section of the CFR, the Perturbation Explanation describes that the original text specifies a standard of maintenance equivalent to Fleet's own equipment, requires sufficient trained personnel, and mandates an annual maintenance plan. The modified text lowers the maintenance standard, makes personnel availability discretionary, and changes the annual plan to a mere 'general overview.' The absence of detailed planning contradicts 21 CFR § 211.180 requirements.

perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it shall not export, directly or indirectly, any technical information acquired from the other party under this Agreement or any products using such technical information to a location or in a manner that at the time of export requires an export licence or other governmental approval, without first obtaining the written consent to do so from the appropriate agency or other governmental entity.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees to use reasonable efforts not to export, directly or indirectly, any technical information acquired from the other party under this Agreement or any products using such technical information to a location or in a manner that at the time of export requires an export licence or other governmental approval, but formal consent is not required.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a strict prohibition on exporting technical information without obtaining necessary licenses and governmental approvals. The modified text weakens this prohibition by introducing a 'reasonable efforts' standard and removing the requirement for formal consent. This creates ambiguity.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 22 U.S. Code § 2778 - Control of arms exports and imports
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 22 U.S. Code § 2778, which regulates the export of defense articles and services, including technical data. By weakening the requirement for obtaining export licenses and approvals, the contract creates a potential violation of export control laws, as 'reasonable efforts' may not suffice to meet the stringent requirements of obtaining proper authorization.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 22 U.S. Code § 2778 - Control of arms exports and imports | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 22 U.S. Code § 2778 - Control of arms exports and imports U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Presidential control of exports and imports of defense articles and services, guidance of policy, etc.; designation of United States Munitions List; issuance of export licenses; negotiations information (1) In furtherance of world peace and the security and foreign policy of the United States, the President is authorized to control the import and the export of defense articles and defense services and to provide foreign policy guidance to persons of the United States involved in the export and import of such articles and services. The President is authorized to designate those items which shall be considered as defense articles and defense services for the purposes of this section and to promulgate regulations for the import and export of such articles and services. The items so designated shall constitute the United States Munitions List. (2) Decisions on issuing export licenses under this section shall take into account whether the export of an article would contribute to an arms race, aid in the development of weapons of mass destruction , support international terrorism, increase the possibility of outbreak or escalation of conflict, or prejudice the development of bilateral or multilateral arms control or nonproliferation agreements or other arrangements. (3) In exercising the authorities conferred by this section, the President may require that any defense article or defense service be sold under this chapter as a condition of its eligibility for export, and may require that persons engaged in the negotiation for the export of defense articles and services keep the President fully and currently informed of the progress and future prospects of such negotiations. (b) Regi
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original strict prohibition on exporting technical information without proper licenses by introducing a 'reasonable efforts' standard and removing the requirement for formal consent. This contradicts 22 U.S. Code § 2778, which controls arms exports and imports. The law states the President is authorized to control the export of defense articles and services and to promulgate regulations for the export of such articles and services (Snippet 2). By suggesting 'reasonable efforts' are sufficient and removing formal consent, the contract undermines the President's authority to control exports and ensure compliance with regulations, therefore, a contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Fleet and Columbia shall maintain comprehensive general liability insurance, including product liability insurance against claims regarding the manufacture of Product under this Agreement and sufficient cover to meet its liabilities under this Agreement in respect of the Columbia Equipment, with insurers having an AM Best rating within the top 2 categories at the time (at the date of this Agreement known as "superior" or "excellent") or reasonably comparable coverage, in such amounts as it customarily maintains for similar products and activities, but in no event less than [***] per individual claim and [***] in the aggregate.
perturbation.changed_text: Fleet and Columbia shall attempt to maintain some general liability insurance, potentially including product liability insurance against claims regarding the manufacture of Product under this Agreement, with insurers as they see fit. There is no specified minimum amount.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause requires comprehensive insurance with highly rated insurers and a minimum coverage amount. The modified clause removes the requirement for comprehensive insurance, eliminates the insurer rating requirement, and removes the minimum coverage amount.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this contract is between a UK and a Bermuda company, and is governed by the laws of England, the Bermuda company needs to maintain insurance that complies with Bermuda law. The modified clause contradicts the intent of insurance regulations like the Insurance Act 1978 (Bermuda), which mandates minimum solvency margins and adequate insurance coverage for companies operating within its jurisdiction. By removing the specific requirements and minimum coverage amounts, the contract fails to ensure compliance with basic insurance standards required for companies registered in Bermuda.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates 'comprehensive' insurance with highly-rated insurers and specific minimum coverage. The changed text weakens this by stating that Fleet and Columbia shall 'attempt to maintain some' insurance 'potentially including' product liability insurance. This removal of the requirement for comprehensive insurance, insurer rating requirements, and minimum coverage amounts directly contradicts the intent of insurance regulations which aims to ensure adequate coverage. The law explanation specifies that the modified clause contradicts the intent of insurance regulations like the Insurance Act 1978 (Bermuda), which mandates minimum solvency margins and adequate insurance coverage for companies operating within its jurisdiction. The absence of a valid scraped snippet, however, weakens this assessment. Based on the provided information, specifically the Law Explanation, the move from 'shall maintain comprehensive' to 'attempt to maintain some' alongside the removal of specific requirements, constitutes a significant weakening of insurance obligations, establishing a legal contradiction under the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction'.
